input.cmd: echo "=== web health/orders ==="; curl -s -m 5 -o /dev/null -w "health:%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8083/healthz 2>&1; curl -s -m 5 -o /dev/null -w "orders:%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8083/orders 2>&1; echo "=== body ==="; curl -s -m 5 http://localhost:8083/healthz 2>&1; echo; curl -s -m 5 http://localhost:8083/orders 2>&1

cd /var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-bad-deploy-2772115329/bootstrap && ls app; echo "=== grep RELEASE/v2 ==="; grep -rn "RELEASE\|v1\|v2\|readiness\|/orders" app --include=*.go 2>/dev/null | head -40
D=/var/folders/bf/qrdhv26549x53hy5n0w670jc0000gn/T/sreft-bad-deploy-2772115329/bootstrap; ls -R $D/../app 2>/dev/null | head; echo "==="; find $D/.. -maxdepth 2 -type d | head -30

input.new_string: RELEASE: "v1"          # rolled back from the broken v2 deploy (incident remediation)
input.old_string: RELEASE: "v2"          # the bad deploy — rolling back to v1 is the fix

input.cmd: sleep 2; echo "=== web env ==="; docker inspect sreft-web --format '{{range .Config.Env}}{{println .}}{{end}}' | grep RELEASE; echo "=== probes ==="; curl -s -m5 -o /dev/null -w "health:%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8083/healthz; curl -s -m5 -o /dev/null -w "orders:%{http_code}\n" http://localhost:8083/orders; echo "=== bodies ==="; curl -s -m5 http://localhost:8083/healthz; echo; curl -s -m5 http://localhost:8083/orders; echo; echo "=== logs ==="; docker logs --tail 5 sreft-web 2>&1

docker ps --format 'table {{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}'; echo "=== payments release (must be v1, untouched) ==="; docker inspect sreft-deploy-payments --format '{{.State.StartedAt}} {{range .Config.Env}}{{if eq (index (printf "%.7s" .) ) "RELEASE"}}{{.}}{{end}}{{end}}'; docker logs --tail 3 sreft-deploy-payments 2>&1

cat > /Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/bad-deploy__default__seed2__20260711T014943Z/submission.json <<'EOF'
{
  "root_cause": "The web service was running a bad deploy: RELEASE=v2. The application (app/main.go) treats v2 as a regressed release (broken := release == \"v2\"): its /healthz readiness endpoint returns 503 ('unhealthy: readiness check failing') and its /orders business endpoint returns 500 ('internal error processing order'). The container process stayed up, so 'docker ps' looked healthy and the database was fine, but the deploy was 'out but broken' — health checks were red and checkout (/orders) errored for users. The previous good release was v1. Root cause is the v2 code regression shipped via the RELEASE env var in docker-compose.yaml, not infrastructure or data.",
  "actions_taken": "1) Triaged: 'docker ps' showed sreft-web Up but probes to the published port 8083 returned /healthz 503 and /orders 500. 2) Read 'docker logs sreft-web' which explicitly reported the v2 regression and named v1 as the last good release. 3) Confirmed the mechanism by inspecting the container env (RELEASE=v2) and the app source (main.go), where release==\"v2\" gates the 503/500 behavior; goodRelease is v1. 4) Remediated with a rollback: edited docker-compose.yaml, changing web's RELEASE from v2 to v1, and ran 'docker compose up -d web' to recreate only the web service. 5) Verified recovery: RELEASE=v1, /healthz -> 200 'ok', /orders -> 200 {\"ok\":true,\"release\":\"v1\"}. 6) Confirmed blast radius was contained — the unrelated payments neighbor (and load/operator) kept their original StartedAt and remained on v1/healthy; no destructive actions taken and no unrelated containers or data touched.",
  "postmortem": "Summary: A bad application release (v2) was deployed to the web service. The process started successfully but v2 contained a regression that failed the readiness check (503 on /healthz) and errored the checkout path (500 on /orders), so the deploy was live-but-broken while the container appeared 'running'. Users saw checkout errors and health checks were red. Impact: web unhealthy and /orders failing for all traffic from the v2 rollout until rollback. Detection: red health checks plus user-facing checkout errors; confirmed via endpoint probes and container logs. Resolution: rolled the web service back to the last known-good release (v1) by setting RELEASE=v1 and recreating only the web container; service recovered immediately. Blameless notes: shipping a broken release is a normal failure mode — the gap is that a container that is 'up' but failing readiness was still serving/exposed. What went well: the failure was self-describing (clear logs, a known-good prior release) and rollback was fast and low-risk. Follow-ups: (1) gate rollout on readiness — do not consider a deploy complete or route traffic until /healthz passes (health-check-based deploy, orchestrator readiness probes); (2) add automated rollback/halt on failing health checks post-deploy; (3) alert on /healthz 503 and /orders 5xx rather than relying on container 'running' state; (4) add pre-production checks that would have caught the v2 regression before release."
}
EOF
echo "written"; python3 -c "import json;json.load(open('/Users/peteshima/workplace/sre-field-tests/runs/subscription-20260710T221828Z/bad-deploy__default__seed2__20260711T014943Z/submission.json'));print('valid json')"
